title: Pressing and drying handmade paper, from wet sheet to finished stock
subtitle: The post-formation steps that determine surface texture and flatness
excerpt: The work doesn't stop at couching. How a handmade sheet is pressed and dried determines its final surface, flatness, and dimensional stability. Over-drying on a warm surface shrinks the sheet; air-drying without restraint produces a buckled, wavy result.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923): Hunter describes the post-pulling process at length, from the spurs used in the traditional paper loft to the board-drying and glass-drying methods used in different European traditions. The post-formation technique here follows the glass-drying method that Hunter documents as producing the flattest, most even sheets for writing and drawing.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stack of alternating wet sheets and felts, built up immediately after couching and ready to go under the press.
The traditional method of hanging wet sheets over a rope in a heated room until air-dry. Produces a slightly wavy sheet without restraint.

content.text: A freshly couched handmade sheet holds around 80% of its weight in water. The pressing stage removes the bulk of this water by compression; the drying stage removes the rest by evaporation. The choice of drying method determines the surface texture: glass-dried sheets are smooth and hard; loft-dried sheets are slightly rough and soft.

items.name: Felts or absorbent cloths
items.notes: Wool felts absorb water well. Old wool blanket or chamois leather cut to A4 size. At least two felts per sheet for the post.

items.name: Pressing boards, two
items.notes: Flat boards that span the post. Plywood or MDF at 15-20 mm, the boards must be flat to avoid imprinting texture on the paper.

items.name: Weight or press
items.notes: A stack of heavy books, concrete blocks, or a simple bolt press (as in the making-a-book-block-press tutorial). Sustained pressure for 15-20 minutes is more effective than brief heavy pressure.

items.name: Glass or smooth board for drying
items.notes: For glass-dried sheets. A clean pane of window glass, a ceramic tile, or a smooth Formica board. The sheet adheres as it dries and peels off when complete.

content.text: Stack the couched sheets as a
: felt, sheet, felt, sheet, and so on. Place a pressing board under the bottom felt and a board on top. The stack should be square, use a corner guide if needed.

Apply weight or press gradually, not all at once. Sudden heavy pressure squeezes the fibres unevenly and can cause the sheet to stick to the felt. Increase the pressure over five minutes. Hold at full pressure for 15-20 minutes. The felts should be visibly damp from water pressed out. Remove the sheets from the felts carefully, they are still fragile.

Lay the pressed sheet on the clean glass or smooth board immediately after removing from the felt. Smooth it gently from the centre outward with a clean sponge or the back of a wide brush, pressing out any air bubbles. Leave to dry at room temperature. As the sheet dries it contracts slightly, pulling itself flat against the glass. The bond releases spontaneously when the sheet is completely dry, test by lifting one corner. If it lifts cleanly, the sheet is done; if it tears slightly, leave it for another 30 minutes.

surface, hang the pressed sheet over a rope or wooden dowel and allow to air-dry without restraint. The free-hanging sheet buckles slightly as it dries. Press it flat under weight for 30 minutes after drying to reduce the wave.
